system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: The Major Scale

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 4 — The Major Scale

_Pages 9-10._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Scale / Mode definition

> The word scale (another term that is used for the word scale is mode) refers to a’ group of notes
> (tones) and the intervals or distances between any 2 adjacent ones.

**Significance (p. 9).** Foundational definition of scale with 'mode' synonym.

## Whole step definition

> Any 2 notes that are 2 frets apart(in other words, separated by 1 fret) are said to be a whole
> step apart.

**Significance (p. 9).** Defines whole step interval in fret terms.

## Half step definition

> Any 2 notes that are adjacent (right
> next to each other) are 4 step apart.

**Significance (p. 9).** Defines half step, completing two-interval vocabulary.

## Major scale interval formula

> The major
> scale has, starting from any note (which will coincide with the name of the scale), a series of two
> whole steps, a 4 step, 3 whole steps, and anothet % step.

**Significance (p. 9).** States the W-W-H-W-W-W-H construction rule for any major scale.

## Root / tonic and octave

> The Ist (and 8th) tone of the

**Significance (p. 9).** Introduces root/tonic and octave terminology.

## Tone numbering system

> The term TONE refers to the number of a note, if you were to number the notes of a major scale
> from 1 to 8. For instance, D is the 4th tone of the A major scale. When talking about these tones, it is
> customary just to say “D is the fourth of A”, or “B is the fifth of A” etc.

**Significance (p. 9).** Establishes numbered-tone shorthand for chord formulas.

## Interval counting rule

> Also the term “a third
> higher” refers to counting 3 tones higher in the scale, including the tone you start from as the Ist
> tone.

**Significance (p. 9).** Clarifies inclusive counting convention.

## Roman numeral chord labeling

> When referring to chords whose roots are based on a major scale, roman numerals are commonly
> used. Example: D is called the IV of A; C¥m is the II minor (Im) of A; E7 is the V7 of A, etc,

**Significance (p. 9).** Introduces Roman numeral harmony notation.

## Chord formula definition

> Every chord has a certain combination of tones that distinguishes it from every other chord. These
> tones may be derived from the major scale with the same root as the chord name.

**Significance (p. 10).** Core principle that chord construction derives from the major scale.

## Triad definition

> The
> particular group of tones that is used for the construction of a particular kind of chord is called the
> FORMULA of that chord; any chord that has 3 tones in the formula is called a TRIAD.

**Significance (p. 10).** Defines 'formula' and 'triad' terms used throughout.

## Chord families: extensions, altered, suspended

> When tones are
> ADDED to any of the 3 basic chords, the resulting chords are called EXTENSIONS. For example:
> Cm7 is an extension of Cm because the bath (BD) is added to the basic Cm chord to make the Cm7. In
> other words, 1, 63, 5, becomes 1, 63, 5,67, Any chord that has a STH, #5TH, DTH or #9TH in the
> formula is called an ALTERED chord; any chord that has the 3RD replaced by a 4TH is called a
> SUSPENDED chord.

**Significance (p. 10).** Taxonomy of extension, altered, and suspended chords.

## Inversions / voicings definition

> Any chord may be played with different arrangements of the tones in the formulas; these different
> ways to play the same chord are called INVERSIONS or VOICINGS.

**Significance (p. 10).** Defines inversions/voicings as equivalent terms.

## String-set inversion system

> starting with a 3 or 4 note chord, RAISE each tone in the chord ON THE SAME STRING up
> to the next higher tone IN THE CHORD.

**Significance (p. 10).** Book's characteristic method for generating chord inversions.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
